author: barnabasbusa

author: potuz
content:
yes, I believe the spec should not deal with testing edge cases. Same problem happens at the fork boundary: the spec for state transition assumes that the state is on a fork and you sync a block from that fork, there's no cross fork checks because it complicates the python impl. Then this means that at the function "upgrade_to_whatever" all these possible edge cases and extra info that may be needed to process the next block, need to be added on that irregular state transition. This is one solution to the problem, but definitely not the unique one, and not even asserted in the spec at all (since there is no state root computation at the fork) so clients can do whatever they want. Python implementation leaks into clients because the spec tests enforce that the upgrade function is computed exactly as the python spec requires. 

So this is the problem of overspecification: that clients are forced to do something that is not necessary and in many places not performant. 

In this particular case this is just a very minor thing so I don't really care if it merges. But all these little leaks of "extra" checks that are irrelevant for mainnet do add up.
